question:
Let $p$ be an odd prime and let $\Z_p$ denote (the field of) integers
modulo $p$. How many elements are in the set
\[
\{x^2: x \in \Z_p\} \cap \{y^2 + 1 : y \in \Z_p\}?
\]
solution:
Solution 1. Let \( S \) be the set of solutions to \( x^{2}=y^{2}+1 \) over \( \mathbb{F}_{p} \). The linear change of coordinates \( (u, v)=(x+y, x-y) \) is invertible since \( \operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cc}1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1\end{array}\right)=-2 \) is nonzero in \( \mathbb{F}_{p} \). Hence \( |S| \) equals the number of solutions to \( u v=1 \) over \( \mathbb{F}_{p} \). There is one possible \( v \) for each nonzero \( u \), and no \( v \) for \( u=0 \), so \( |S|=p-1 \).

The problem asks for the size of the image of the map \( \phi: S \rightarrow \mathbb{F}_{p} \) taking \( (x, y) \) to \( x^{2} \). If \( z=x^{2} \) for some \( (x, y) \in S \), then \( \phi^{-1}(z)=\{( \pm x, \pm y)\} \), which has size 4 , except in the cases \( z=1 \) (in which case \( x= \pm 1 \) and \( y=0 \), making \( \phi^{-1}(z) \) of size 2 ) and \( z=0 \) (in which case \( x=0 \) and \( y^{2}=-1 \), again making \( \phi^{-1}(z) \) of size 2); the latter exception occurs if and only if -1 is a square in \( \mathbb{F}_{p} \). Hence \( |S|=4|\phi(S)|-2-2 c \), where \( c \) is 1 or 0 according as -1 is a square in \( \mathbb{F}_{p} \) or not. Thus the answer to the problem is
\[
|\phi(S)|=\frac{(p-1)+2+2 c}{4}=\frac{p+1+2 c}{4} .
\]

This should be an integer, so \( c=1 \) if \( p \equiv 1(\bmod 4) \) and \( c=0 \) if \( p \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \). In either case, \( |\phi(S)|=\lceil p / 4\rceil \) as claimed.

Solution 2. We use the Legendre symbol, defined by
\[
\left(\frac{a}{p}\right)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
1, & \text { if } a \equiv k^{2} \quad(\bmod p) \text { for some } k \not \equiv 0 \quad(\bmod p) \\
0, & \text { if } a \equiv 0 \quad(\bmod p) \\
-1, & \text { otherwise }
\end{array}\right.
\]

The number of nonzero squares in \( \mathbb{F}_{p} \) equals the number of nonsquares, so
\[
\sum_{a=0}^{p-1}\left(\frac{a-k}{p}\right)=0
\]
for any \( k \in \mathbb{Z} \).
In this solution only, if \( P \) is a statement, let \( [P] \) be 1 if \( P \) is true, and 0 if \( P \) is false. Let \( \mathbb{F}_{p}^{2} \) denote the set of squares in \( \mathbb{F}_{p} \), including 0 . The problem asks us to compute
\[
N=\sum_{a=0}^{p-1}\left[a \in \mathbb{F}_{p}^{2}\right] \cdot\left[a-1 \in \mathbb{F}_{p}^{2}\right]
\]

Substituting the identity
\[
\left[a \in \mathbb{F}_{p}^{2}\right]=\frac{1}{2}\left(1+\left(\frac{a}{p}\right)+[a=0]\right)
\]
we obtain
\[
\begin{aligned}
N & =\frac{1}{4}\left(1+\left(\frac{-1}{p}\right)+1+\left(\frac{1}{p}\right)+\sum_{a=0}^{p-1}\left(1+\left(\frac{a}{p}\right)\right)\left(1+\left(\frac{a-1}{p}\right)\right)\right) \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{-1}{p}\right)=1\right]+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4} \sum_{a=0}^{p-1}\left(1+\left(\frac{a}{p}\right)+\left(\frac{a-1}{p}\right)+\left(\frac{a}{p}\right)\left(\frac{a-1}{p}\right)\right) \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{-1}{p}\right)=1\right]+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{p}{4}+\frac{1}{4} \sum_{a=0}^{p-1}\left(\frac{a}{p}\right)\left(\frac{a-1}{p}\right),
\end{aligned}
\]
by (1) twice. For \( k \in \mathbb{Z} \), let \( S(k)=\sum_{a=0}^{p-1}\left(\frac{a}{p}\right)\left(\frac{a-k}{p}\right) \). We want \( S(1) \). For \( k \) not divisible by \( p \), the substitution \( a=k b \), with \( b \) running over the residue classes modulo \( p \), shows that
\[
\begin{aligned}
S(k) & =\sum_{b=0}^{p-1}\left(\frac{k}{p}\right)\left(\frac{b}{p}\right)\left(\frac{k}{p}\right)\left(\frac{b-1}{p}\right) \\
& =\sum_{b=0}^{p-1}\left(\frac{b}{p}\right)\left(\frac{b-1}{p}\right) \quad\left(\text { since }\left(\frac{k}{p}\right)^{2}=1\right) \\
& =S(1)
\end{aligned}
\]

Also,
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{p-1} S(k)=\sum_{a=0}^{p-1}\left(\frac{a}{p}\right) \sum_{k=0}^{p-1}\left(\frac{a-k}{p}\right)=0
\]
since each inner sum is zero by (1). Thus
\[
S(1)=-\frac{S(0)}{p-1}=-\frac{p-1}{p-1}=-1
\]

Hence
\[
N=\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{-1}{p}\right)=1\right]+\frac{p+1}{4} .
\]

But \( N \) is an integer. Thus if \( p \equiv 1(\bmod 4) \), then \( \left(\frac{-1}{p}\right)=1 \) and \( N=(p+3) / 4 \); if \( p \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \), then \( \left(\frac{-1}{p}\right)=-1 \) and \( N=(p+1) / 4 \).

descriptive_long.question:
Let $primep$ be an odd prime and let $integersmodprime$ denote (the field of) integers modulo $primep$. How many elements are in the set
\[
\{coordx^2: coordx \in integersmodprime\} \cap \{coordy^2 + 1 : coordy \in integersmodprime\}?
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution 1. Let \( solutionset \) be the set of solutions to \( coordx^{2}=coordy^{2}+1 \) over \( \mathbb{F}_{primep} \). The linear change of coordinates \( (coordu, coordv)=(coordx+coordy, coordx-coordy) \) is invertible since \( \operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cc}1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1\end{array}\right)=-2 \) is nonzero in \( \mathbb{F}_{primep} \). Hence \( |solutionset| \) equals the number of solutions to \( coordu \, coordv=1 \) over \( \mathbb{F}_{primep} \). There is one possible \( coordv \) for each nonzero \( coordu \), and no \( coordv \) for \( coordu=0 \), so \( |solutionset|=primep-1 \).

The problem asks for the size of the image of the map \( \phi: solutionset \rightarrow \mathbb{F}_{primep} \) taking \( (coordx, coordy) \) to \( coordx^{2} \). If \( coordz=coordx^{2} \) for some \( (coordx, coordy) \in solutionset \), then \( \phi^{-1}(coordz)=\{( \pm coordx, \pm coordy)\} \), which has size 4 , except in the cases \( coordz=1 \) (in which case \( coordx= \pm 1 \) and \( coordy=0 \), making \( \phi^{-1}(coordz) \) of size 2 ) and \( coordz=0 \) (in which case \( coordx=0 \) and \( coordy^{2}=-1 \), again making \( \phi^{-1}(coordz) \) of size 2); the latter exception occurs if and only if -1 is a square in \( \mathbb{F}_{primep} \). Hence \( |solutionset|=4|\phi(solutionset)|-2-2 parityflag \), where \( parityflag \) is 1 or 0 according as -1 is a square in \( \mathbb{F}_{primep} \) or not. Thus the answer to the problem is
\[
|\phi(solutionset)|=\frac{(primep-1)+2+2 parityflag}{4}=\frac{primep+1+2 parityflag}{4} .
\]

This should be an integer, so \( parityflag=1 \) if \( primep \equiv 1(\bmod 4) \) and \( parityflag=0 \) if \( primep \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \). In either case, \( |\phi(solutionset)|=\lceil primep / 4\rceil \) as claimed.

Solution 2. We use the Legendre symbol, defined by
\[
\left(\frac{elementa}{primep}\right)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
1, & \text { if } elementa \equiv elementk^{2} \quad(\bmod primep) \text { for some } elementk \not \equiv 0 \quad(\bmod primep) \\
0, & \text { if } elementa \equiv 0 \quad(\bmod primep) \\
-1, & \text { otherwise }
\end{array}\right.
\]

The number of nonzero squares in \( \mathbb{F}_{primep} \) equals the number of nonsquares, so
\[
\sum_{elementa=0}^{primep-1}\left(\frac{elementa-elementk}{primep}\right)=0
\]
for any \( elementk \in \mathbb{Z} \).
In this solution only, if \( predicatep \) is a statement, let \( [predicatep] \) be 1 if \( predicatep \) is true, and 0 if \( predicatep \) is false. Let \( \mathbb{F}_{primep}^{2} \) denote the set of squares in \( \mathbb{F}_{primep} \), including 0 . The problem asks us to compute
\[
countn=\sum_{elementa=0}^{primep-1}\left[elementa \in \mathbb{F}_{primep}^{2}\right] \cdot\left[elementa-1 \in \mathbb{F}_{primep}^{2}\right]
\]

Substituting the identity
\[
\left[elementa \in \mathbb{F}_{primep}^{2}\right]=\frac{1}{2}\left(1+\left(\frac{elementa}{primep}\right)+[elementa=0]\right)
\]
we obtain
\[
\begin{aligned}
countn & =\frac{1}{4}\left(1+\left(\frac{-1}{primep}\right)+1+\left(\frac{1}{primep}\right)+\sum_{elementa=0}^{primep-1}\left(1+\left(\frac{elementa}{primep}\right)\right)\left(1+\left(\frac{elementa-1}{primep}\right)\right)\right) \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{-1}{primep}\right)=1\right]+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4} \sum_{elementa=0}^{primep-1}\left(1+\left(\frac{elementa}{primep}\right)+\left(\frac{elementa-1}{primep}\right)+\left(\frac{elementa}{primep}\right)\left(\frac{elementa-1}{primep}\right)\right) \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{-1}{primep}\right)=1\right]+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{primep}{4}+\frac{1}{4} \sum_{elementa=0}^{primep-1}\left(\frac{elementa}{primep}\right)\left(\frac{elementa-1}{primep}\right),
\end{aligned}
\]
by (1) twice. For \( elementk \in \mathbb{Z} \), let \( solutionset(elementk)=\sum_{elementa=0}^{primep-1}\left(\frac{elementa}{primep}\right)\left(\frac{elementa-elementk}{primep}\right) \). We want \( solutionset(1) \). For \( elementk \) not divisible by \( primep \), the substitution \( elementa=elementk elementb \), with \( elementb \) running over the residue classes modulo \( primep \), shows that
\[
\begin{aligned}
solutionset(elementk) & =\sum_{elementb=0}^{primep-1}\left(\frac{elementk}{primep}\right)\left(\frac{elementb}{primep}\right)\left(\frac{elementk}{primep}\right)\left(\frac{elementb-1}{primep}\right) \\
& =\sum_{elementb=0}^{primep-1}\left(\frac{elementb}{primep}\right)\left(\frac{elementb-1}{primep}\right) \quad\left(\text { since }\left(\frac{elementk}{primep}\right)^{2}=1\right) \\
& =solutionset(1)
\end{aligned}
\]

Also,
\[
\sum_{elementk=0}^{primep-1} solutionset(elementk)=\sum_{elementa=0}^{primep-1}\left(\frac{elementa}{primep}\right) \sum_{elementk=0}^{primep-1}\left(\frac{elementa-elementk}{primep}\right)=0
\]
since each inner sum is zero by (1). Thus
\[
solutionset(1)=-\frac{solutionset(0)}{primep-1}=-\frac{primep-1}{primep-1}=-1
\]

Hence
\[
countn=\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{-1}{primep}\right)=1\right]+\frac{primep+1}{4} .
\]

But \( countn \) is an integer. Thus if \( primep \equiv 1(\bmod 4) \), then \( \left(\frac{-1}{primep}\right)=1 \) and \( countn=(primep+3) / 4 \); if \( primep \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \), then \( \left(\frac{-1}{primep}\right)=-1 \) and \( countn=(primep+1) / 4 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $lighthouse$ be an odd prime and let $chocolate$ denote (the field of) integers modulo $lighthouse$. How many elements are in the set
\[
\{sunflower^2: sunflower \in chocolate\} \cap \{blackberry^2 + 1 : blackberry \in chocolate\}?\
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution 1. Let \( watermelon \) be the set of solutions to \( sunflower^{2}=blackberry^{2}+1 \) over \( marshmallow \). The linear change of coordinates \( (butterfly, dragonfly)=(sunflower+blackberry, sunflower-blackberry) \) is invertible since \( \operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cc}1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1\end{array}\right)=-2 \) is nonzero in \( marshmallow \). Hence \( |watermelon| \) equals the number of solutions to \( butterfly \, dragonfly=1 \) over \( marshmallow \). There is one possible \( dragonfly \) for each nonzero \( butterfly \), and no \( dragonfly \) for \( butterfly=0 \), so \( |watermelon|=lighthouse-1 \).

The problem asks for the size of the image of the map \( \phi: watermelon \rightarrow marshmallow \) taking \( (sunflower, blackberry) \) to \( sunflower^{2} \). If \( rhinoceros=sunflower^{2} \) for some \( (sunflower, blackberry) \in watermelon \), then \( \phi^{-1}(rhinoceros)=\{( \pm sunflower, \pm blackberry)\} \), which has size 4 , except in the cases \( rhinoceros=1 \) (in which case \( sunflower= \pm 1 \) and \( blackberry=0 \), making \( \phi^{-1}(rhinoceros) \) of size 2 ) and \( rhinoceros=0 \) (in which case \( sunflower=0 \) and \( blackberry^{2}=-1 \), again making \( \phi^{-1}(rhinoceros) \) of size 2); the latter exception occurs if and only if -1 is a square in \( marshmallow \). Hence \( |watermelon|=4|\phi(watermelon)|-2-2 caterpillar \), where \( caterpillar \) is 1 or 0 according as -1 is a square in \( marshmallow \) or not. Thus the answer to the problem is
\[
|\phi(watermelon)|=\frac{(lighthouse-1)+2+2 caterpillar}{4}=\frac{lighthouse+1+2 caterpillar}{4} .
\]

This should be an integer, so \( caterpillar=1 \) if \( lighthouse \equiv 1(\bmod 4) \) and \( caterpillar=0 \) if \( lighthouse \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \). In either case, \( |\phi(watermelon)|=\lceil lighthouse / 4\rceil \) as claimed.

Solution 2. We use the Legendre symbol, defined by
\[
\left(\frac{pineapple}{lighthouse}\right)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
1, & \text { if } pineapple \equiv strawberry^{2} \quad(\bmod lighthouse) \text { for some } strawberry \not \equiv 0 \quad(\bmod lighthouse) \\
0, & \text { if } pineapple \equiv 0 \quad(\bmod lighthouse) \\
-1, & \text { otherwise }
\end{array}\right.
\]

The number of nonzero squares in \( marshmallow \) equals the number of nonsquares, so
\[
\sum_{pineapple=0}^{lighthouse-1}\left(\frac{pineapple-raspberry}{lighthouse}\right)=0
\]
for any \( raspberry \in \mathbb{Z} \).
In this solution only, if \( honeycomb \) is a statement, let \( [honeycomb] \) be 1 if \( honeycomb \) is true, and 0 if \( honeycomb \) is false. Let \( marshmallow^{2} \) denote the set of squares in \( marshmallow \), including 0 . The problem asks us to compute
\[
blueberry=\sum_{pineapple=0}^{lighthouse-1}\left[pineapple \in marshmallow^{2}\right] \cdot\left[pineapple-1 \in marshmallow^{2}\right]
\]

Substituting the identity
\[
\left[pineapple \in marshmallow^{2}\right]=\frac{1}{2}\left(1+\left(\frac{pineapple}{lighthouse}\right)+[pineapple=0]\right)
\]
we obtain
\[
\begin{aligned}
blueberry & =\frac{1}{4}\left(1+\left(\frac{-1}{lighthouse}\right)+1+\left(\frac{1}{lighthouse}\right)+\sum_{pineapple=0}^{lighthouse-1}\left(1+\left(\frac{pineapple}{lighthouse}\right)\right)\left(1+\left(\frac{pineapple-1}{lighthouse}\right)\right)\right) \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{-1}{lighthouse}\right)=1\right]+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4} \sum_{pineapple=0}^{lighthouse-1}\left(1+\left(\frac{pineapple}{lighthouse}\right)+\left(\frac{pineapple-1}{lighthouse}\right)+\left(\frac{pineapple}{lighthouse}\right)\left(\frac{pineapple-1}{lighthouse}\right)\right) \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{-1}{lighthouse}\right)=1\right]+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{lighthouse}{4}+\frac{1}{4} \sum_{pineapple=0}^{lighthouse-1}\left(\frac{pineapple}{lighthouse}\right)\left(\frac{pineapple-1}{lighthouse}\right),
\end{aligned}
\]
by (1) twice. For \( raspberry \in \mathbb{Z} \), let \( watermelon(raspberry)=\sum_{pineapple=0}^{lighthouse-1}\left(\frac{pineapple}{lighthouse}\right)\left(\frac{pineapple-raspberry}{lighthouse}\right) \). We want \( watermelon(1) \). For \( raspberry \) not divisible by \( lighthouse \), the substitution \( pineapple=raspberry \, strawberry \), with \( strawberry \) running over the residue classes modulo \( lighthouse \), shows that
\[
\begin{aligned}
watermelon(raspberry) & =\sum_{strawberry=0}^{lighthouse-1}\left(\frac{raspberry}{lighthouse}\right)\left(\frac{strawberry}{lighthouse}\right)\left(\frac{raspberry}{lighthouse}\right)\left(\frac{strawberry-1}{lighthouse}\right) \\
& =\sum_{strawberry=0}^{lighthouse-1}\left(\frac{strawberry}{lighthouse}\right)\left(\frac{strawberry-1}{lighthouse}\right) \quad\left(\text { since }\left(\frac{raspberry}{lighthouse}\right)^{2}=1\right) \\
& =watermelon(1)
\end{aligned}
\]

Also,
\[
\sum_{raspberry=0}^{lighthouse-1} watermelon(raspberry)=\sum_{pineapple=0}^{lighthouse-1}\left(\frac{pineapple}{lighthouse}\right) \sum_{raspberry=0}^{lighthouse-1}\left(\frac{pineapple-raspberry}{lighthouse}\right)=0
\]
since each inner sum is zero by (1). Thus
\[
watermelon(1)=-\frac{watermelon(0)}{lighthouse-1}= -\frac{lighthouse-1}{lighthouse-1}=-1
\]

Hence
\[
blueberry=\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{-1}{lighthouse}\right)=1\right]+\frac{lighthouse+1}{4} .
\]

But \( blueberry \) is an integer. Thus if \( lighthouse \equiv 1(\bmod 4) \), then \( \left(\frac{-1}{lighthouse}\right)=1 \) and \( blueberry=(lighthouse+3) / 4 \); if \( lighthouse \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \), then \( \left(\frac{-1}{lighthouse}\right)=-1 \) and \( blueberry=(lighthouse+1) / 4 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $composite$ be an odd prime and let $\realfield$ denote (the field of) integers modulo $composite$. How many elements are in the set
\[
\{knownval^2: knownval \in \realfield\} \cap \{givenval^2 + 1 : givenval \in \realfield\}?
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution 1. Let \( emptiness \) be the set of solutions to \( knownval^{2}=givenval^{2}+1 \) over \( \mathbb{complexset}_{composite} \). The linear change of coordinates \( (downelem, reststate)=(knownval+givenval, knownval-givenval) \) is invertible since \( \operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cc}1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1\end{array}\right)=-2 \) is nonzero in \( \mathbb{complexset}_{composite} \). Hence \( |emptiness| \) equals the number of solutions to \( downelem\, reststate=1 \) over \( \mathbb{complexset}_{composite} \). There is one possible \( reststate \) for each nonzero \( downelem \), and no \( reststate \) for \( downelem=0 \), so \( |emptiness|=composite-1 \).

The problem asks for the size of the image of the map \( \phi: emptiness \rightarrow \mathbb{complexset}_{composite} \) taking \( (knownval, givenval) \) to \( knownval^{2} \). If \( baseline=knownval^{2} \) for some \( (knownval, givenval) \in emptiness \), then \( \phi^{-1}(baseline)=\{( \pm knownval, \pm givenval)\} \), which has size 4, except in the cases \( baseline=1 \) (in which case \( knownval= \pm 1 \) and \( givenval=0 \), making \( \phi^{-1}(baseline) \) of size 2) and \( baseline=0 \) (in which case \( knownval=0 \) and \( givenval^{2}=-1 \), again making \( \phi^{-1}(baseline) \) of size 2); the latter exception occurs if and only if -1 is a square in \( \mathbb{complexset}_{composite} \). Hence \( |emptiness|=4|\phi(emptiness)|-2-2 uncertain \), where \( uncertain \) is 1 or 0 according as -1 is a square in \( \mathbb{complexset}_{composite} \) or not. Thus the answer to the problem is
\[
|\phi(emptiness)|=\frac{(composite-1)+2+2 uncertain}{4}=\frac{composite+1+2 uncertain}{4} .
\]

This should be an integer, so \( uncertain=1 \) if \( composite \equiv 1(\bmod 4) \) and \( uncertain=0 \) if \( composite \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \). In either case, \( |\phi(emptiness)|=\lceil composite / 4\rceil \) as claimed.

Solution 2. We use the Legendre symbol, defined by
\[
\left(\frac{specificval}{composite}\right)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
1, & \text { if } specificval \equiv knownval^{2} \quad(\bmod composite) \text { for some } knownval \not \equiv 0 \quad(\bmod composite) \\
0, & \text { if } specificval \equiv 0 \quad(\bmod composite) \\
-1, & \text { otherwise }
\end{array}\right.
\]

The number of nonzero squares in \( \mathbb{complexset}_{composite} \) equals the number of nonsquares, so
\[
\sum_{specificval=0}^{composite-1}\left(\frac{specificval-staticindex}{composite}\right)=0
\]
for any \( staticindex \in \mathbb{Z} \).
In this solution only, if \( falsehood \) is a statement, let \( [falsehood] \) be 1 if \( falsehood \) is true, and 0 if \( falsehood \) is false. Let \( \mathbb{complexset}_{composite}^{2} \) denote the set of squares in \( \mathbb{complexset}_{composite} \), including 0. The problem asks us to compute
\[
uncounted=\sum_{specificval=0}^{composite-1}\left[specificval \in \mathbb{complexset}_{composite}^{2}\right] \cdot \left[specificval-1 \in \mathbb{complexset}_{composite}^{2}\right]
\]

Substituting the identity
\[
\left[specificval \in \mathbb{complexset}_{composite}^{2}\right]=\frac{1}{2}\left(1+\left(\frac{specificval}{composite}\right)+[specificval=0]\right)
\]
we obtain
\[
\begin{aligned}
uncounted &= \frac{1}{4}\left(1+\left(\frac{-1}{composite}\right)+1+\left(\frac{1}{composite}\right)+\sum_{specificval=0}^{composite-1}\left(1+\left(\frac{specificval}{composite}\right)\right)\left(1+\left(\frac{specificval-1}{composite}\right)\right)\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{-1}{composite}\right)=1\right]+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4} \sum_{specificval=0}^{composite-1}\left(1+\left(\frac{specificval}{composite}\right)+\left(\frac{specificval-1}{composite}\right)+\left(\frac{specificval}{composite}\right)\left(\frac{specificval-1}{composite}\right)\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{-1}{composite}\right)=1\right]+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{composite}{4}+\frac{1}{4} \sum_{specificval=0}^{composite-1}\left(\frac{specificval}{composite}\right)\left(\frac{specificval-1}{composite}\right),
\end{aligned}
\]
by (1) twice. For \( staticindex \in \mathbb{Z} \), let \( emptiness(staticindex)=\sum_{specificval=0}^{composite-1}\left(\frac{specificval}{composite}\right)\left(\frac{specificval-staticindex}{composite}\right) \). We want \( emptiness(1) \). For \( staticindex \) not divisible by \( composite \), the substitution \( specificval=staticindex\,universal \), with \( universal \) running over the residue classes modulo \( composite \), shows that
\[
\begin{aligned}
emptiness(staticindex) &= \sum_{universal=0}^{composite-1}\left(\frac{staticindex}{composite}\right)\left(\frac{universal}{composite}\right)\left(\frac{staticindex}{composite}\right)\left(\frac{universal-1}{composite}\right) \\
&= \sum_{universal=0}^{composite-1}\left(\frac{universal}{composite}\right)\left(\frac{universal-1}{composite}\right) \quad\left(\text { since } \left(\frac{staticindex}{composite}\right)^{2}=1 \right) \\
&= emptiness(1)
\end{aligned}
\]

Also,
\[
\sum_{staticindex=0}^{composite-1} emptiness(staticindex)=\sum_{specificval=0}^{composite-1}\left(\frac{specificval}{composite}\right) \sum_{staticindex=0}^{composite-1}\left(\frac{specificval-staticindex}{composite}\right)=0
\]
since each inner sum is zero by (1). Thus
\[
emptiness(1)=-\frac{emptiness(0)}{composite-1}=-\frac{composite-1}{composite-1}=-1
\]

Hence
\[
uncounted=\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{-1}{composite}\right)=1\right]+\frac{composite+1}{4} .
\]

But \( uncounted \) is an integer. Thus if \( composite \equiv 1(\bmod 4) \), then \( \left(\frac{-1}{composite}\right)=1 \) and \( uncounted=(composite+3) / 4 \); if \( composite \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \), then \( \left(\frac{-1}{composite}\right)=-1 \) and \( uncounted=(composite+1) / 4 \).

garbled_string.question:
Let $lskdjfgh$ be an odd prime and let $\hqkwmsuf$ denote (the field of) integers
modulo $lskdjfgh$. How many elements are in the set
\[
\{wptzskya^2: wptzskya \in \hqkwmsuf\} \cap \{nlbycgev^2 + 1 : nlbycgev \in \hqkwmsuf\}?
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution 1. Let \( xhvdmteq \) be the set of solutions to \( wptzskya^{2}=nlbycgev^{2}+1 \) over \( \mathbb{kjdhsfla} \). The linear change of coordinates \( (ykcjlsmp, dfrnqxoh)=(wptzskya+nlbycgev, wptzskya-nlbycgev) \) is invertible since \( \operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cc}1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1\end{array}\right)=-2 \) is nonzero in \( \mathbb{kjdhsfla} \). Hence \( |xhvdmteq| \) equals the number of solutions to \( ykcjlsmp dfrnqxoh=1 \) over \( \mathbb{kjdhsfla} \). There is one possible \( dfrnqxoh \) for each nonzero \( ykcjlsmp \), and no \( dfrnqxoh \) for \( ykcjlsmp=0 \), so \( |xhvdmteq|=lskdjfgh-1 \).

The problem asks for the size of the image of the map \( \phi: xhvdmteq \rightarrow \mathbb{kjdhsfla} \) taking \( (wptzskya, nlbycgev) \) to \( wptzskya^{2} \). If \( brfajkwi=wptzskya^{2} \) for some \( (wptzskya, nlbycgev) \in xhvdmteq \), then \( \phi^{-1}(brfajkwi)=\{( \pm wptzskya, \pm nlbycgev)\} \), which has size 4 , except in the cases \( brfajkwi=1 \) (in which case \( wptzskya= \pm 1 \) and \( nlbycgev=0 \), making \( \phi^{-1}(brfajkwi) \) of size 2 ) and \( brfajkwi=0 \) (in which case \( wptzskya=0 \) and \( nlbycgev^{2}=-1 \), again making \( \phi^{-1}(brfajkwi) \) of size 2); the latter exception occurs if and only if -1 is a square in \( \mathbb{kjdhsfla} \). Hence \( |xhvdmteq|=4|\phi(xhvdmteq)|-2-2 slnvocwe \), where \( slnvocwe \) is 1 or 0 according as -1 is a square in \( \mathbb{kjdhsfla} \) or not. Thus the answer to the problem is
\[
|\phi(xhvdmteq)|=\frac{(lskdjfgh-1)+2+2 slnvocwe}{4}=\frac{lskdjfgh+1+2 slnvocwe}{4} .
\]

This should be an integer, so \( slnvocwe=1 \) if \( lskdjfgh \equiv 1(\bmod 4) \) and \( slnvocwe=0 \) if \( lskdjfgh \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \). In either case, \( |\phi(xhvdmteq)|=\lceil lskdjfgh / 4\rceil \) as claimed.

Solution 2. We use the Legendre symbol, defined by
\[
\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp}{lskdjfgh}\right)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
1, & \text { if } qzxwvtnp \equiv mvcqsdfa^{2} \quad(\bmod lskdjfgh) \text { for some } mvcqsdfa \not \equiv 0 \quad(\bmod lskdjfgh) \\
0, & \text { if } qzxwvtnp \equiv 0 \quad(\bmod lskdjfgh) \\
-1, & \text { otherwise }
\end{array}\right.
\]

The number of nonzero squares in \( \mathbb{kjdhsfla} \) equals the number of nonsquares, so
\[
\sum_{qzxwvtnp=0}^{lskdjfgh-1}\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp-mvcqsdfa}{lskdjfgh}\right)=0
\]
for any \( mvcqsdfa \in \mathbb{Z} \).
In this solution only, if \( sdbgkzru \) is a statement, let \( [sdbgkzru] \) be 1 if \( sdbgkzru \) is true, and 0 if \( sdbgkzru \) is false. Let \( \mathbb{kjdhsfla}^{2} \) denote the set of squares in \( \mathbb{kjdhsfla} \), including 0 . The problem asks us to compute
\[
ptlgwrhz=\sum_{qzxwvtnp=0}^{lskdjfgh-1}\left[qzxwvtnp \in \mathbb{kjdhsfla}^{2}\right] \cdot\left[qzxwvtnp-1 \in \mathbb{kjdhsfla}^{2}\right]
\]

Substituting the identity
\[
\left[qzxwvtnp \in \mathbb{kjdhsfla}^{2}\right]=\frac{1}{2}\left(1+\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp}{lskdjfgh}\right)+[qzxwvtnp=0]\right)
\]
we obtain
\[
\begin{aligned}
ptlgwrhz & =\frac{1}{4}\left(1+\left(\frac{-1}{lskdjfgh}\right)+1+\left(\frac{1}{lskdjfgh}\right)+\sum_{qzxwvtnp=0}^{lskdjfgh-1}\left(1+\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp}{lskdjfgh}\right)\right)\left(1+\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp-1}{lskdjfgh}\right)\right)\right) \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{-1}{lskdjfgh}\right)=1\right]+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{lskdjfgh}{4}+\frac{1}{4} \sum_{qzxwvtnp=0}^{lskdjfgh-1}\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp}{lskdjfgh}\right)\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp-1}{lskdjfgh}\right),
\end{aligned}
\]
by (1) twice. For \( mvcqsdfa \in \mathbb{Z} \), let \( xhvdmteq(mvcqsdfa)=\sum_{qzxwvtnp=0}^{lskdjfgh-1}\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp}{lskdjfgh}\right)\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp-mvcqsdfa}{lskdjfgh}\right) \). We want \( xhvdmteq(1) \). For \( mvcqsdfa \) not divisible by \( lskdjfgh \), the substitution \( qzxwvtnp=mvcqsdfa hjgrksla \), with \( hjgrksla \) running over the residue classes modulo \( lskdjfgh \), shows that
\[
\begin{aligned}
xhvdmteq(mvcqsdfa) & =\sum_{hjgrksla=0}^{lskdjfgh-1}\left(\frac{mvcqsdfa}{lskdjfgh}\right)\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{lskdjfgh}\right)\left(\frac{mvcqsdfa}{lskdjfgh}\right)\left(\frac{hjgrksla-1}{lskdjfgh}\right) \\
& =\sum_{hjgrksla=0}^{lskdjfgh-1}\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{lskdjfgh}\right)\left(\frac{hjgrksla-1}{lskdjfgh}\right) \quad\left(\text { since }\left(\frac{mvcqsdfa}{lskdjfgh}\right)^{2}=1\right) \\
& =xhvdmteq(1)
\end{aligned}
\]

Also,
\[
\sum_{mvcqsdfa=0}^{lskdjfgh-1} xhvdmteq(mvcqsdfa)=\sum_{qzxwvtnp=0}^{lskdjfgh-1}\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp}{lskdjfgh}\right) \sum_{mvcqsdfa=0}^{lskdjfgh-1}\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp-mvcqsdfa}{lskdjfgh}\right)=0
\]
since each inner sum is zero by (1). Thus
\[
xhvdmteq(1)=-\frac{xhvdmteq(0)}{lskdjfgh-1}=-\frac{lskdjfgh-1}{lskdjfgh-1}=-1
\]

Hence
\[
ptlgwrhz=\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{-1}{lskdjfgh}\right)=1\right]+\frac{lskdjfgh+1}{4} .
\]

But \( ptlgwrhz \) is an integer. Thus if \( lskdjfgh \equiv 1(\bmod 4) \), then \( \left(\frac{-1}{lskdjfgh}\right)=1 \) and \( ptlgwrhz=(lskdjfgh+3) / 4 \); if \( lskdjfgh \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \), then \( \left(\frac{-1}{lskdjfgh}\right)=-1 \) and \( ptlgwrhz=(lskdjfgh+1) / 4 \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let $q$ be an odd prime power and write $\Bbb F_{q}$ for the finite field with $q$ elements.  
Fix two distinct non-zero elements $\alpha ,\beta \in\Bbb F_{q}^{\times}$ with $\alpha\neq\beta$ and set
\[
\mathcal S_{0}= \{x^{2}:x\in\Bbb F_{q}\},\qquad
\mathcal S_{\alpha}= \{y^{2}+\alpha :y\in\Bbb F_{q}\},\qquad
\mathcal S_{\beta}= \{z^{2}+\beta :z\in\Bbb F_{q}\}.
\]

Denote by $\chi:\Bbb F_{q}\longrightarrow\{0,\pm1\}$ the quadratic (multiplicative) character extended by $\chi(0)=0$, put $\varepsilon=\chi(-1)$ and write  
\[
a_q(\alpha,\beta)=q+1-\#E_{\alpha,\beta}(\Bbb F_{q}),\qquad 
E_{\alpha,\beta} :\; Y^{2}=X(X-\alpha)(X-\beta).
\]

(a)  Show that the triple intersection  
\[
N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)=\bigl|\mathcal S_{0}\cap\mathcal S_{\alpha}\cap\mathcal S_{\beta}\bigr|
\]
admits the closed formula  
\[
N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)=
\frac{q-a_{q}(\alpha,\beta)
       +(1+\varepsilon)\bigl(\chi(\alpha)+\chi(\beta)+\chi(\alpha-\beta)\bigr)
       +\chi(\alpha)\chi(\beta)
       +\chi(\alpha)\chi(\alpha-\beta)
       +\varepsilon\,\chi(\beta)\chi(\alpha-\beta)}{8}.
\tag{$\star$}
\]

(b)  Prove that the right-hand side of $(\star)$ is an integer and that it satisfies  
\[
0\;\le\;N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)\;\le\;q .
\]

No information other than $q$, $\varepsilon$, $\chi(\alpha)$, $\chi(\beta)$, $\chi(\alpha-\beta)$ and $a_q(\alpha,\beta)$ may appear in the final expression.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout let $\chi:\Bbb F_{q}\to\{0,\pm1\}$ be the quadratic character, $\chi(0)=0$, and put $\varepsilon=\chi(-1)\in\{\pm1\}$.

1.  Indicator reformulation.  
Define  
\[
I(u)=\begin{cases}1,&u\in\mathcal S_{0},\\[2pt]0,&u\notin\mathcal S_{0},\end{cases}
\qquad 
N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)=\sum_{t\in\Bbb F_{q}}I(t)\,I(t-\alpha)\,I(t-\beta).
\]

2.  Character expansion of $I$.  
Because $0$ is a square,
\[
I(u)=\frac{1+\chi(u)}{2}+\frac{\delta(u)}{2},\qquad 
\delta(u)=\begin{cases}1,&u=0,\\[2pt]0,&u\neq0.\end{cases}
\]

3.  Separation into generic and exceptional arguments.  
The factors $t$, $t-\alpha$, $t-\beta$ can vanish only for $t\in\{0,\alpha,\beta\}$.  Put  
\[
A=\Bbb F_{q}\setminus\{0,\alpha,\beta\},\qquad 
N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)=N_{\operatorname{gen}}+N_{\operatorname{exc}} .
\]

4.  Generic contribution.  
For $t\in A$ all $\delta$-terms disappear:
\[
I(t)I(t-\alpha)I(t-\beta)=\frac18\bigl(1+\chi(t)\bigr)\bigl(1+\chi(t-\alpha)\bigr)\bigl(1+\chi(t-\beta)\bigr).
\]
Expanding yields the sums
\[
\Sigma_0=q-3,\qquad
\Sigma_1=\sum_{t\in A}\bigl(\chi(t)+\chi(t-\alpha)+\chi(t-\beta)\bigr),
\]
\[
\Sigma_2=\sum_{t\in A}\bigl(\chi(t)\chi(t-\alpha)+\chi(t)\chi(t-\beta)+\chi(t-\alpha)\chi(t-\beta)\bigr),
\qquad
\Sigma_3=\sum_{t\in A}\chi\!\bigl(t(t-\alpha)(t-\beta)\bigr).
\]

4(a)  Linear sums.  
Because $\sum_{x\in\Bbb F_{q}}\chi(x)=0$ and translating variables does not change the sum, one finds
\[
\Sigma_1=-(1+\varepsilon)\bigl(\chi(\alpha)+\chi(\beta)+\chi(\alpha-\beta)\bigr).
\tag{4.1}
\]

4(b)  Quadratic sums - a correct evaluation of the Jacobi sum.  
For $\gamma\neq0$ define  
\[
J(\gamma)=\sum_{x\in\Bbb F_{q}}\chi(x)\chi(x-\gamma).
\]
The change of variables $x=\gamma u$ gives $J(\gamma)=J(1)$, so $J(\gamma)$
is independent of $\gamma\neq0$.  
To determine $J(1)$ count the $\Bbb F_{q}$-points on the conic
\[
C:\qquad Y^{2}=X(X-1).
\]
For $X=0$ or $X=1$ there is exactly one $Y$, while for
$X\notin\{0,1\}$ the fibre contains $1+\chi(X(X-1))$ points.
Hence
\[
\#C(\Bbb F_{q})=2+\sum_{X\notin\{0,1\}}\bigl(1+\chi(X)\chi(X-1)\bigr)
                =q+J(1).
\]
Since $C$ is a nonsingular genus-$0$ curve, it is birational to
$\Bbb P^{1}$ and therefore $\#C(\Bbb F_{q})=q+1$; hence $J(1)=-1$.
Consequently
\[
J(\gamma)=-1\qquad(\gamma\neq0).
\tag{4.2}
\]

Removing the three exceptional arguments ($t=0,\alpha,\beta$) gives
\[
\Sigma_2=-3-\Bigl[\chi(\alpha)\chi(\beta)+\chi(\alpha)\chi(\alpha-\beta)
                +\varepsilon\,\chi(\beta)\chi(\alpha-\beta)\Bigr].
\tag{4.3}
\]

4(c)  Cubic sum via the elliptic curve.  
Because $\chi(0)=0$, deleting $t\in\{0,\alpha,\beta\}$ does not affect
\[
\Sigma_3=\sum_{t\in\Bbb F_{q}}\chi\!\bigl(t(t-\alpha)(t-\beta)\bigr)=:S(\alpha,\beta).
\]
For any $x\in\Bbb F_{q}$ the equation $y^{2}=x(x-\alpha)(x-\beta)$
has exactly $1+\chi\!\bigl(x(x-\alpha)(x-\beta)\bigr)$ solutions in $y$,
whence
\[
\#E_{\alpha,\beta}(\Bbb F_{q})=q+1+S(\alpha,\beta),
\qquad S(\alpha,\beta)=-a_q(\alpha,\beta).
\tag{4.4}
\]

4(d)  Collecting the generic part.  
Inserting (4.1)-(4.4) yields
\[
N_{\operatorname{gen}}
=\frac18\Bigl[
         q-6-a_q(\alpha,\beta)
        -(1+\varepsilon)\!\bigl(\chi(\alpha)+\chi(\beta)+\chi(\alpha-\beta)\bigr)
        -\bigl(\chi(\alpha)\chi(\beta)+\chi(\alpha)\chi(\alpha-\beta)
               +\varepsilon\,\chi(\beta)\chi(\alpha-\beta)\bigr)
      \Bigr].
\tag{4.5}
\]

5.  Exceptional contribution.  
Evaluating $I$ at $t=0,\alpha,\beta$ gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
t=0:&\quad 
P_{0}=\dfrac{\bigl(1+\varepsilon\chi(\alpha)\bigr)\bigl(1+\varepsilon\chi(\beta)\bigr)}{4},\\[6pt]
t=\alpha:&\quad 
P_{\alpha}=\dfrac{\bigl(1+\chi(\alpha)\bigr)\bigl(1+\chi(\alpha-\beta)\bigr)}{4},\\[6pt]
t=\beta:&\quad 
P_{\beta}=\dfrac{\bigl(1+\chi(\beta)\bigr)\bigl(1+\varepsilon\chi(\alpha-\beta)\bigr)}{4}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus $N_{\operatorname{exc}}=P_{0}+P_{\alpha}+P_{\beta}$ and
\[
2\,N_{\operatorname{exc}}
      =\frac{3+(1+\varepsilon)\bigl(\chi(\alpha)+\chi(\beta)+\chi(\alpha-\beta)\bigr)
              +\chi(\alpha)\chi(\beta)
              +\chi(\alpha)\chi(\alpha-\beta)
              +\varepsilon\,\chi(\beta)\chi(\alpha-\beta)}{2}.
\tag{5.1}
\]

6.  Final combination.  
Adding (4.5) and $\tfrac12\,$(5.1) gives
\[
N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)=
\frac{q-a_q(\alpha,\beta)
       +(1+\varepsilon)\bigl(\chi(\alpha)+\chi(\beta)+\chi(\alpha-\beta)\bigr)
       +\chi(\alpha)\chi(\beta)
       +\chi(\alpha)\chi(\alpha-\beta)
       +\varepsilon\,\chi(\beta)\chi(\alpha-\beta)}{8},
\]
exactly the announced formula $(\star)$.

7.  Integrality of $(\star)$.  
All manipulations from Section 1 to Section 6 were carried out inside
$\Bbb Z$; in particular we established the equality
\[
8\,N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)=
q-a_q(\alpha,\beta)
+(1+\varepsilon)\bigl(\chi(\alpha)+\chi(\beta)+\chi(\alpha-\beta)\bigr)
+\chi(\alpha)\chi(\beta)
+\chi(\alpha)\chi(\alpha-\beta)
+\varepsilon\,\chi(\beta)\chi(\alpha-\beta).
\]
Because the left-hand side is an integer, the numerator of $(\star)$
is automatically a multiple of $8$, so $(\star)\in\Bbb Z$ and
$N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)$ is an integer.

8.  Non-negativity and the upper bound.  

8(a)  Range of the character expression.  
Let $A,B,C\in\{\pm1\}$ and denote $s=A+B+C$, $p=AB+AC+BC$.
A short inspection shows  
\[
-3\le (1+\varepsilon)s+p\le 9
\quad\text{and}\quad
-3\le p\le 3.
\tag{8.1}
\]

8(b)  Use of Hasse's bound.  
The Hasse-Weil inequality $|a_q(\alpha,\beta)|\le 2\sqrt q$ together
with (8.1) furnishes  
\[
q-2\sqrt q-3\le 
q-a_q(\alpha,\beta)+(1+\varepsilon)s+p\le q+2\sqrt q+9 .
\]
For $q\ge9$ the left-hand side is non-negative, while the right-hand
side is $\le 8q$; dividing by $8$ yields $0\le N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)\le q$.

8(c)  Small fields $q=3,5,7$.  
Because at most $q$ elements can lie in the intersection of three
subsets of $\Bbb F_{q}$, the upper bound is automatic.
Direct substitution of the eight possible triples
$(\chi(\alpha),\chi(\beta),\chi(\alpha-\beta))$
shows non-negativity of $(\star)$ for $q=3,5,7$ as well.

Thus $(\star)$ is integral and
\[
0\;\le\;N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)\;\le\;q .
\]

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Higher dimensional intersection:  the original problem intersects two quadratic images; the enhanced variant intersects three, creating a cubic condition.
2.  Advanced machinery:  evaluation of the cubic character sum is translated into counting $\mathbb F_q$-points on an elliptic curve, introducing algebraic–geometry (elliptic curves, Frobenius trace, Hasse bound).
3.  Non-elementary constants:  the answer involves the Frobenius trace $a_q(\alpha,\beta)$, a subtle arithmetic invariant that generally cannot be expressed by elementary Legendre symbols alone.
4.  Multiple interacting concepts:  quadratic characters, Jacobi sums, elliptic curves, and the correction for the special value $0$ all interact.
5.  Longer derivation:  the solution requires partitioning the sum, expanding products, using classical character-sum identities, and invoking elliptic-curve point counting—considerably deeper and more elaborate than the linear change-of-variables trick that solved the original exercise.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $q$ be an odd prime power and write $\Bbb F_{q}$ for the finite field with $q$ elements.  
Fix two distinct non-zero elements $\alpha,\beta\in\Bbb F_{q}^{\times}$ with $\alpha\neq\beta$ and set  
\[
\mathcal S_{0}= \{x^{2}:x\in\Bbb F_{q}\},\qquad
\mathcal S_{\alpha}= \{y^{2}+\alpha :y\in\Bbb F_{q}\},\qquad
\mathcal S_{\beta}= \{z^{2}+\beta :z\in\Bbb F_{q}\}.
\]

Denote by $\chi:\Bbb F_{q}\to\{0,\pm1\}$ the quadratic (multiplicative) character extended by $\chi(0)=0$, put $\varepsilon=\chi(-1)$ and write  
\[
a_q(\alpha,\beta)=q+1-\#E_{\alpha,\beta}(\Bbb F_{q}),\qquad 
E_{\alpha,\beta} :\; Y^{2}=X(X-\alpha)(X-\beta).
\]

Determine a closed formula for  
\[
N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)=\bigl|\mathcal S_{0}\cap\mathcal S_{\alpha}\cap\mathcal S_{\beta}\bigr|
\]
expressed solely through $q$, $\varepsilon$, $\chi(\alpha)$, $\chi(\beta)$, $\chi(\alpha-\beta)$ and $a_q(\alpha,\beta)$.  
Prove that your expression is an integer satisfying $0\le N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)\le q$.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout let $\chi:\Bbb F_{q}\to\{0,\pm1\}$ be the quadratic character with $\chi(0)=0$ and put $\varepsilon=\chi(-1)\in\{\pm1\}$.

1.\;Indicator reformulation.  
Write the indicator of squares as  
\[
I(u)=\begin{cases}1,&u\in\mathcal S_{0},\\[2pt]0,&u\notin\mathcal S_{0}.\end{cases}
\qquad 
N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)=\sum_{t\in\Bbb F_{q}}I(t)\,I(t-\alpha)\,I(t-\beta).
\]

2.\;Character expansion of $I$.  
Because $0$ is a square while $\chi(0)=0$,
\[
I(u)=\frac{1+\chi(u)}{2}+\frac{\delta(u)}{2},\qquad 
\delta(u)=\begin{cases}1,&u=0,\\[2pt]0,&u\neq0.\end{cases}
\]

3.\;Generic versus exceptional arguments.  
The arguments $t$, $t-\alpha$, $t-\beta$ vanish precisely when $t\in\{0,\alpha,\beta\}$.  
Put  
\[
A=\Bbb F_{q}\setminus\{0,\alpha,\beta\},\qquad 
N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)=N_{\mathrm{gen}}+N_{\mathrm{exc}} .
\]

4.\;Generic contribution.  
For $t\in A$ all $\delta$-terms disappear, giving  
\[
I(t)I(t-\alpha)I(t-\beta)=\frac18\bigl(1+\chi(t)\bigr)\bigl(1+\chi(t-\alpha)\bigr)\bigl(1+\chi(t-\beta)\bigr).
\]
Expanding produces three character sums

\[
\Sigma_1=\sum_{t\in A}\bigl(\chi(t)+\chi(t-\alpha)+\chi(t-\beta)\bigr),
\]
\[
\Sigma_2=\sum_{t\in A}\bigl(\chi(t)\chi(t-\alpha)+\chi(t)\chi(t-\beta)+\chi(t-\alpha)\chi(t-\beta)\bigr),
\]
\[
\Sigma_3=\sum_{t\in A}\chi\!\bigl(t(t-\alpha)(t-\beta)\bigr).
\]

4(a) Constant term: $q-3$.

4(b) Linear sums.  Using $\sum_{x\in\Bbb F_{q}}\chi(x)=0$ and translating variables,
\[
\Sigma_1=-(1+\varepsilon)\bigl(\chi(\alpha)+\chi(\beta)+\chi(\alpha-\beta)\bigr).
\]

4(c) Quadratic sums.  For $\gamma\neq0$ one has
\[
\sum_{x\in\Bbb F_{q}}\chi(x)\chi(x-\gamma)=-1 .
\]
Removing the exceptional points gives exactly
\[
\Sigma_2=-3-\Bigl[\chi(\alpha)\chi(\beta)+\chi(\alpha)\chi(\alpha-\beta)
                +\varepsilon\,\chi(\beta)\chi(\alpha-\beta)\Bigr].
\]

4(d) Cubic sum.  Since $\chi(0)=0$, deleting the three exceptional $t$ does not alter the value:
\[
\Sigma_3=\sum_{t\in\Bbb F_{q}}\chi\!\bigl(t(t-\alpha)(t-\beta)\bigr)=:S(\alpha,\beta).
\]

Collecting,
\[
N_{\mathrm{gen}}
      =\frac18\Bigl[(q-3)+\Sigma_1+\Sigma_2+\Sigma_3\Bigr].
\tag{1}
\]

5.\;Elliptic-curve interpretation of $S(\alpha,\beta)$.  
For any $x\in\Bbb F_{q}$ the equation $y^{2}=x(x-\alpha)(x-\beta)$ has $1+\chi\!\bigl(x(x-\alpha)(x-\beta)\bigr)$ solutions in $y$.  Summing over $x$ gives
\[
\#E_{\alpha,\beta}(\Bbb F_{q})=q+1+S(\alpha,\beta),
\]
hence
\[
S(\alpha,\beta)=-a_q(\alpha,\beta).
\]
Insert into (1):

\[
N_{\mathrm{gen}}
   =\frac18\Bigl[
        q-6-a_q(\alpha,\beta)
       -(1+\varepsilon)\!\bigl(\chi(\alpha)+\chi(\beta)+\chi(\alpha-\beta)\bigr)
       -\bigl(\chi(\alpha)\chi(\beta)+\chi(\alpha)\chi(\alpha-\beta)
              +\varepsilon\,\chi(\beta)\chi(\alpha-\beta)\bigr)
     \Bigr].
\tag{2}
\]

6.\;Exceptional contribution.  
Evaluate $I$ at $t=0,\alpha,\beta$ (note $\delta(\alpha)=\delta(\beta)=0$):

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
t=0: & P_{0}=\dfrac{\bigl(1+\varepsilon\chi(\alpha)\bigr)\bigl(1+\varepsilon\chi(\beta)\bigr)}{4},\\[8pt]
t=\alpha: & P_{\alpha}=\dfrac{\bigl(1+\chi(\alpha)\bigr)\bigl(1+\chi(\alpha-\beta)\bigr)}{4},\\[8pt]
t=\beta: & P_{\beta}=\dfrac{\bigl(1+\chi(\beta)\bigr)\bigl(1+\varepsilon\chi(\alpha-\beta)\bigr)}{4}.
\end{array}
\]
Hence
\[
N_{\mathrm{exc}}=P_{0}+P_{\alpha}+P_{\beta}.
\tag{3}
\]

7.\;Final combination.  
Write
\[
\Sigma_1'=\chi(\alpha)+\chi(\beta)+\chi(\alpha-\beta),\qquad
\Sigma_2'=\chi(\alpha)\chi(\beta)+\chi(\alpha)\chi(\alpha-\beta)
          +\varepsilon\,\chi(\beta)\chi(\alpha-\beta).
\]
A short calculation from (3) shows
\[
2\,N_{\mathrm{exc}}=\frac{3+(1+\varepsilon)\Sigma_1'+\Sigma_2'}{2}.
\tag{4}
\]
Adding (2) and (3) and simplifying with (4) yields
\[
\boxed{\,N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)=
       \frac{q-a_q(\alpha,\beta)
              +(1+\varepsilon)\Sigma_1'
              +\Sigma_2'}{8}\,}.
\tag{5}
\]

Explicitly,
\[
N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)=
\frac{q-a_q(\alpha,\beta)
       +(1+\varepsilon)\bigl(\chi(\alpha)+\chi(\beta)+\chi(\alpha-\beta)\bigr)
       +\chi(\alpha)\chi(\beta)
       +\chi(\alpha)\chi(\alpha-\beta)
       +\varepsilon\,\chi(\beta)\chi(\alpha-\beta)}{8}.
\]

8.\;Integrality.  
Formula (5) arose by summing integer quantities and dividing exactly once by $8$.  
Each intermediate coefficient of $\tfrac18$ in (1) originates from
eight equal terms and is therefore already an integer; likewise $N_{\mathrm{exc}}$ is a sum of three quarter-integers, hence an element of $\tfrac14\Bbb Z$.  
Consequently the numerator in (5) is a multiple of $8$, so $N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)\in\Bbb Z$.

9.\;Automatic non-negativity and upper bound.  
Because (5) equals the cardinality $N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)$ found at the outset, it is automatically $\ge 0$ and $\le q$.  
Nevertheless one can verify it directly from the formula:

(i) Range of the character part.  
For $A,B,C\in\{\pm1\}$ set  
$s=A+B+C$, $p=AB+AC+BC$.  One checks the pairwise possibilities to see  
$-3\le 2s+p\le 9$ and $-3\le p\le 3$.  Hence
\[
-3\le(1+\varepsilon)\Sigma_1'+\Sigma_2'\le 9 .
\]

(ii) Hasse's bound $|a_q(\alpha,\beta)|\le2\sqrt q$ then gives
\[
q-2\sqrt q-3\;\le\; 
q-a_q(\alpha,\beta)+(1+\varepsilon)\Sigma_1'+\Sigma_2'
\;\le\; 
q+2\sqrt q+9 .
\]
For $q\ge9$ the left-hand side is non-negative; for $q=3,5,7$ a short
direct inspection of (5) (or explicit counting) confirms
$N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)\ge0$.  In every case $N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)\le q$
because it is the count of elements of a subset of $\Bbb F_{q}$.

10.\;Numerical check.  
Take $q=7$, $\alpha=1$, $\beta=2$.  Then
$\varepsilon=-1$, $\chi(\alpha)=\chi(\beta)=1$, $\chi(\alpha-\beta)=-1$ and
$\#E_{1,2}(\Bbb F_{7})=8$, so $a_{7}(1,2)=0$.  Formula (5) gives
\[
N_{1,2}(7)=\frac{7-0+(1-1)(1+1-1)+(1-1+1)}{8}=1,
\]
matching a direct enumeration.

Hence (5) completes the determination of $N_{\alpha,\beta}(q)$.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Higher dimensional intersection:  the original problem intersects two quadratic images; the enhanced variant intersects three, creating a cubic condition.
2.  Advanced machinery:  evaluation of the cubic character sum is translated into counting $\mathbb F_q$-points on an elliptic curve, introducing algebraic–geometry (elliptic curves, Frobenius trace, Hasse bound).
3.  Non-elementary constants:  the answer involves the Frobenius trace $a_q(\alpha,\beta)$, a subtle arithmetic invariant that generally cannot be expressed by elementary Legendre symbols alone.
4.  Multiple interacting concepts:  quadratic characters, Jacobi sums, elliptic curves, and the correction for the special value $0$ all interact.
5.  Longer derivation:  the solution requires partitioning the sum, expanding products, using classical character-sum identities, and invoking elliptic-curve point counting—considerably deeper and more elaborate than the linear change-of-variables trick that solved the original exercise.
